Prompt:
[ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment. Read the patient case and follow the instructions to provide a diagnosis. Note: Not all patients will have a diagnosis.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral: KF is a 38-year-old structural engineer who referred himself to the local community mental health center. He complains of difficulty sleeping over the past 2 weeks that has made it difficult for him to focus at work.
Presenting Symptoms:  KF presents to the interview looking tired but otherwise well groomed. He reports that he has been waking up in the middle of the night each night for the past 2 weeks and has difficulty falling back asleep. As a result, he says that he feels like he is dragging all day, having a very hard time focusing on what he is supposed to be doing. Because his work is so detail-oriented, he worries that he will make a mistake that could cause serious problems for the bridge he is designing. He says he feels uptight and tense at work, constantly running over in his mind the ways he could make a mistake but also feeling generally keyed up without being able to say why. KF says that he has a hard time relaxing when he goes home and his stomach is upset the whole evening. He usually enjoys watching television in the evenings or playing video games, but recently he cannot seem to muster the energy to do so. Rather, those things just do not seem as fun as they used to be. His wife is getting frustrated that he is letting his list of chores pile up, because he complains that he is too tired to do any of them. His eyes moisten as he says he is beginning to feel like his life is starting to fall apart at home and at work. He has been late in completing two assignments in the past week, although his supervisor has given him a grace period to get caught up. His mounting problems led him to think he needs some help, leading to the current visit.
Additional Background Information:  KF has been happily married for the past 15 years. Up until this point of his life, he has always performed very well at work. He has no psychiatric history, and reports that he has never before experienced a period of time during which he has felt similarly. He reports that he does not use alcohol or any other drugs. No abnormal findings that could account for his current condition were detected in the physical examinations at his initial visit to the clinic.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
Please carefully read the patient case and follow these steps:
1. Review the symptoms, their severity, their duration, whether they have occurred before (recurrence), and impact on functioning
2. Review the list below of potential diagnoses from ICD11, focusing on understanding the key differences between each diagnosis
3. Select the 3 most likely diagnoses from the list below in descending order, keeping in mind that the person may not have any diagnosis
3. Provide a brief explanation (1 sentences) for why these diagnoses best fit the case, citing specific symptoms and criteria

Potential mood-related diagnoses (ICD11):
- Single Episode Depressive Disorder
- Recurrent Depressive Disorder
- Dysthymic Disorder
- Mixed Depressive and Anxiety Disorder
- Bipolar Type I Disorder
- Bipolar Type II Disorder
- Cyclothymic Disorder
- Other Mood Disorder
- Generalized Anxiety Disorder
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- Other Diagnosis
- No Diagnosis

[FORMAT]
Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Second Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

**WARNING:** Any other output format is incorrect.
Model_Output:
Most Likely Diagnosis: Adjustment Disorder with Depressed Mood
Reasoning: The patient's symptoms of insomnia, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, and decreased interest in activities are consistent with adjustment disorder with depressed mood. These symptoms are a normal response to a stressor, in this case, the stressors at work and home.

Second Most Likely: Insomnia Disorder
Reasoning: The patient's primary complaint is his inability to sleep, which is causing significant impairment in his daily functioning. However, the presence of other symptoms such as difficulty concentrating and decreased interest in activities suggests that insomnia is not the sole diagnosis.

Third Most Likely: Recurrent Depressive Disorder
Reasoning: The patient's history of performing well at work and having no previous psychiatric history makes recurrent depressive disorder less likely than other diagnoses. However, the severity and duration of his current symptoms, as well as the impact on his functioning, are consistent with this diagnosis.
Model_Diagnoses: ['Adjustment Disorder with Depressed Mood', 'Insomnia Disorder', 'Recurrent Depressive Disorder']

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral: RK is a 50-year-old engineer who was referred because of intense feelings of despair. He sought consultation because he could not shake feelings of lethargy and self-loathing that plague him most of the day. He lost his wife in a car accident 5 months ago but says he had already come to terms with her death prior to becoming overwhelmed with his current feelings. He has two adult children who live nearby with their own families.
Presenting Symptoms:  RK took some time off work after the death of his wife, but returned to work about 3 months ago. For about the past month, he has been feeling extremely low. Usually a gregarious and enthusiastic person, he feels empty and sad. Even his most entertaining friends cannot coax him to go out. In fact, he says he is resentful of people who try to encourage him because it makes him feel worse.  Over the past month, RK has stopped spending time with his grown children and grandchildren because these interactions have become difficult and exhausting. He has been making excuses to get out of seeing them as much as possible, which has caused both of his children to express concern. RK finds their questions annoying even though he knows they have good intentions, so he has stopped answering the telephone when they call. RK’s productivity at work has declined markedly over the past month, to the point that his supervisor has told him that he is at risk for losing his job in a company where he worked for more than 20 years with regular promotions. He says he has difficulty concentrating and often finds himself reading the same page over and over, and has also been feeling that his work is pointless and trivial. At home, he finds himself watching television shows without being able to follow the storyline. RK says he cannot get interested in doing anything or in going anywhere, and that he lacks energy and motivation to do even small tasks around the house, something that has always been a source of satisfaction for him. RK reports that he has gained approximately 4 kg (9 lbs.) over the past month, and attributes this change to his being so much less active than usual. RK states that he is exhausted. He has difficulty falling asleep, wakes several times during the night and he does not feel rested or refreshed after the few hours of sleep that he is able to get. During the day he feels restless and on edge, but also like he is moving in slow motion. He reports that his limbs feel like lead weights. He also says that he has a “short fuse” recently.  For example, 2 weeks ago, during one of the rare occasions when he did see his family, he snapped at his 5-year-old granddaughter and made her cry when she was just “being a kid.” RK says he looks back on his life and thinks he never really accomplished anything and that he has nothing to look forward to in the future. He recognizes that his thoughts occasionally drift to memories of times when his wife was present, but he says that his feelings about his life have nothing to do with her. He says that he has a strong desire to “go to sleep and not wake up” and thinks that everyone would be better off if this were to occur. He thinks frequently about overdosing on the diazepam (Valium) that was prescribed to him by his family physician.
Additional Background Information:  RK recounted that he has experienced similar periods of sadness earlier in his life. Once as a teenager and several times in adulthood, he has experienced prolonged periods of lethargy and apathy often lasting a month or two, during which he lost his appetite, could not seem to focus, and was unusually critical of himself. RK has never experienced any periods of increased excitement or energy lasting as long as a week. He met his wife at the beginning of his senior year in college and they were married 8 months later. However, they often quarreled over her abuse of alcohol. His wife was intoxicated when she drove her car into a tree 5 months ago. RK states that he misses her but he feels angry that she was so irresponsible and guilty that he could not convince her to stop drinking. While RK also used to drink with his wife when they were in their 20s, he saw “what it was doing to her” and stopped. He does not currently use alcohol or any other drugs. A medical exam has ruled out other medical conditions that could be causing his symptoms.
